title: redcore OS Playbook
shortDescription: Windows optimization playbook for redcore OS
description: A review-first Windows optimization playbook that stages cleanup, privacy hardening, and performance tuning through a guided wizard.
details: redcore OS scans the current install, resolves a profile-aware plan, and packages the same decisions for wizard and ISO flows.

bulletPoints.title: Lower Latency
bulletPoints.description: Stages the same timer, power, and scheduler optimizations exposed by the desktop wizard.

bulletPoints.title: Telemetry Control
bulletPoints.description: Removes the worst consumer tracking defaults without pretending every enterprise feature can disappear.

bulletPoints.title: Rollback Ready
bulletPoints.description: Keeps rollback metadata so the plan can be audited and reversed instead of acting like a blind tweak pack.

desktopQuestions.label: Optimization Level
desktopQuestions.title: How much should we optimize?
desktopQuestions.desc: This decides how many changes we make. Start safe, or go deeper if you know what you're doing.
desktopQuestions.note: If unsure, pick Balanced. You can always revisit.

options.title: Conservative
options.desc: Light cleanup and privacy fixes only. Nothing risky. Best if you're not sure.

options.title: Balanced
options.desc: Good mix of performance and privacy improvements. Works well for most PCs.

options.title: Aggressive
options.desc: Deeper tweaks for gaming PCs and power users. May change how some features work.

options.title: Expert
options.desc: Unlocks all options including security tradeoffs. Only for users who know exactly what they want.

desktopQuestions.label: Power Plan
desktopQuestions.title: Use the High Performance power plan?
desktopQuestions.desc: This stops Windows from slowing down your CPU to save power. Your PC will respond faster. Sets powercfg to High Performance scheme and adjusts processor idle states.
desktopQuestions.note: Sets GUID 8c5e7fda-e8bf-4a96-9a85-a6e23a8c635c. This is the single most impactful change for desktop performance.

options.title: Yes — use High Performance
options.desc: CPU stays at higher frequencies. Eliminates 1-5ms wake latency from C-states. Measurable in frame time graphs.

options.title: No — keep Windows power defaults
options.desc: Keeps Balanced plan with CPU frequency scaling. Better for laptops on battery.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Windows balanced power behavior.

desktopQuestions.label: CPU Priority
desktopQuestions.title: Give more CPU power to the app you're using?
desktopQuestions.desc: Adjusts Win32PrioritySeparation to favor foreground processes. Makes the scheduler give your active app (game, editor) more quantum time than background services.
desktopQuestions.note: Registry: HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\PriorityControl. Research-backed by valleyofdoom/PC-Tuning.

options.title: Yes — prioritize the active app
options.desc: Sets Win32PrioritySeparation=0x26 (short, variable, foreground boost). Measurable improvement in input-to-frame latency.

options.title: No — keep default sharing
options.desc: Keeps default Win32PrioritySeparation. Background tasks get equal scheduling.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep default Windows thread scheduling behavior.

desktopQuestions.label: Timer Fix
desktopQuestions.title: Fix the Windows timer for better game performance?
desktopQuestions.desc: Windows 11 changed timer resolution to per-process (24H2+). This sets GlobalTimerResolutionRequests=1 to restore the global 0.5ms timer that games rely on for consistent frame pacing.
desktopQuestions.note: Registry: HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager\kernel. Only effective on Win11 22H2+ / Server 2022+.

options.title: Yes — restore global timer behavior
options.desc: Sets kernel GlobalTimerResolutionRequests=1. Reduces timer jitter from ~15.6ms to ~0.5ms. Directly improves frame pacing.

options.title: No — keep modern per-app timer
options.desc: Keeps per-process timer. Some games may have inconsistent frame times.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep modern per-process timer behavior.

desktopQuestions.label: Search Indexing
desktopQuestions.title: Turn off Windows Search indexing?
desktopQuestions.desc: WSearch service constantly scans files in the background. Disabling it stops the indexer, reduces disk I/O, and frees ~100-200MB RAM.
desktopQuestions.note: Service: WSearch. Also disables SearchIndexer.exe. You can still search — just without instant results.

options.title: Yes — disable indexing
options.desc: Stops WSearch service (Start=4). Saves CPU, disk I/O, and RAM. Search still works, just slower.

options.title: No — keep indexing enabled
options.desc: Keeps fast file search in Start menu and Explorer. Uses background CPU/disk.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Windows Search indexing enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: Search Cleanup
desktopQuestions.title: Clean up the Start menu search?
desktopQuestions.desc: Removes web results, search highlights, and the oversized search bar from the taskbar.
desktopQuestions.note: This is intentionally more honest than claiming complete search removal when the actual playbook applies targeted shell/search actions.

options.title: Yes — local search only
options.desc: Search only shows your local files and apps. No web clutter.

options.title: No — keep full search
options.desc: Keeps web suggestions and the full search experience.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Windows Search web features and history behavior.

desktopQuestions.label: Printing
desktopQuestions.title: Do you use a printer?
desktopQuestions.desc: If you don't print, we can safely disable the print service. This also removes a known security vulnerability.
desktopQuestions.note: This question is preservation-driven: the answer directly decides whether the spooler disable action is blocked or applied.

options.title: Yes — I use a printer
options.desc: Printer support stays enabled.

behavior.blockReason: You said this machine still needs printer support.

options.title: No — I don't print
options.desc: Disables the print service. Frees resources and improves security.

desktopQuestions.label: Remote Access
desktopQuestions.title: Do you need remote desktop access?
desktopQuestions.desc: Remote Desktop lets others control your PC over the network. If nobody needs to access this PC remotely, we can disable it for better security.
desktopQuestions.note: This is the kind of preservation decision that should be explicit before apply, not discovered after something breaks.

options.title: Yes — I need remote access
options.desc: Keeps remote desktop and assistance tools working.

behavior.blockReason: You said this machine still needs remote access or remote support.

options.title: No — nobody accesses this PC remotely
options.desc: Disables remote access. Reduces your attack surface.

desktopQuestions.label: Edge Browser
desktopQuestions.title: What should we do with Microsoft Edge's background activity?
desktopQuestions.desc: Edge runs 3-5 background processes even when closed, pre-loads at boot, and nags you to switch from your preferred browser. It's like that friend who won't leave your party.
desktopQuestions.note: Disables msedge startup boost, browser replacement prompts, and update tasks. Most people should pick 'Suppress'.

options.title: Keep Edge unchanged
options.desc: Leave Edge completely untouched.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Microsoft Edge behavior unchanged.

options.title: Suppress background preload and default-browser nags
options.desc: Stop Edge from loading at startup and showing 'switch to Edge' popups.

behavior.blockReason: You chose not to freeze Edge updates.

options.title: Suppress Edge and also freeze auto-updates
options.desc: Also prevents Edge from updating itself. You'd need to update it manually.

desktopQuestions.label: Copilot
desktopQuestions.title: Remove Windows Copilot?
desktopQuestions.desc: Copilot is Microsoft's AI assistant wedged into the taskbar. Uses 150-300MB RAM sitting idle and phones home to Bing. Nobody asked for this.

options.title: Yes — remove Copilot
options.desc: Disables Copilot via TurnOffWindowsCopilot policy. Frees ~200MB RAM. Your taskbar can finally breathe.

options.title: No — keep Copilot available
options.desc: Keeps Copilot available on the taskbar. It's actually useful sometimes... apparently.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Copilot available.

desktopQuestions.label: Recall
desktopQuestions.title: Disable Windows Recall?
desktopQuestions.desc: Recall takes screenshots of your screen every few seconds so AI can search your history. Yes, really. It stores everything locally but the concept is... concerning.
desktopQuestions.note: Only shown on supported Windows builds where Recall actually exists.

options.title: Yes — disable Recall
options.desc: Stops the screenshot-based activity tracking completely. Your screen is yours again.

options.title: No — keep Recall available
options.desc: Keeps Recall active. Microsoft pinky-promises the data stays local.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Recall available.

desktopQuestions.label: Click To Do
desktopQuestions.title: Disable Click to Do?
desktopQuestions.desc: Click to Do is a Windows 11 AI feature that analyzes what's on your screen and suggests actions.
desktopQuestions.note: Only shown on supported Windows builds where Click to Do is present.

options.title: Yes — disable it
options.desc: Removes this AI overlay. Less clutter.

options.title: No — keep it available
options.desc: Keeps Click to Do available.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Click to Do available.

desktopQuestions.label: AI In Apps
desktopQuestions.title: Disable AI features in Edge, Paint, and Notepad?
desktopQuestions.desc: Microsoft added AI to several built-in apps. These send your data to the cloud for processing.

options.title: Yes — disable app AI features
options.desc: Turns off AI features in these apps. Your data stays local.

options.title: No — keep AI features
options.desc: Keeps AI features available in built-in apps.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep AI features in system apps.

desktopQuestions.label: Data Collection
desktopQuestions.title: How much should we reduce Microsoft's data collection?
desktopQuestions.desc: Windows sends usage data, app crash reports, typing/inking data, and browsing patterns to Microsoft via DiagTrack service and scheduled tasks.
desktopQuestions.note: Disables DiagTrack service, CEIP, ErrorReporting, PushNotifications, and cloud content. AllowTelemetry=0 only fully works on Enterprise/EDU editions.

options.title: Keep Windows defaults
options.desc: Leave all data collection at Windows defaults. DiagTrack stays running.

behavior.blockReason: You chose not to reduce Microsoft telemetry in this area.

options.title: Reduce data collection
options.desc: Disables DiagTrack, CEIP, ErrorReporting, ProgramDataUpdater, and Consolidator tasks. Sets AllowTelemetry=0.

behavior.blockReason: You chose the lighter telemetry reduction profile.

options.title: Maximum privacy
options.desc: Everything above + blocks feedback requests, advertising ID, activity feed, inking data, and cloud content suggestions.

desktopQuestions.label: Clipboard
desktopQuestions.title: Disable clipboard history and cloud sync?
desktopQuestions.desc: Windows can save everything you copy and sync it across devices. If you don't use this, it's just extra tracking.

options.title: Yes — disable clipboard history/sync
options.desc: Clipboard works normally but doesn't save history or sync.

options.title: No — keep clipboard history
options.desc: Keeps clipboard history and cross-device sync.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep clipboard history and sync capability.

desktopQuestions.label: Activity Tracking
desktopQuestions.title: Disable activity tracking?
desktopQuestions.desc: Windows tracks what files you open and apps you use, then shows this in a 'timeline'. This data is also sent to the cloud.

options.title: Yes — disable it
options.desc: Stops Windows from tracking and uploading your activity.

options.title: No — keep it
options.desc: Keeps the activity timeline and cloud sync.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep activity feed or cloud content behavior.

desktopQuestions.label: Location
desktopQuestions.title: Disable location tracking?
desktopQuestions.desc: Windows tracks your location for maps, weather, and Find My Device. On a desktop, this is usually unnecessary.

options.title: Yes — disable location
options.desc: Turns off location services and Find My Device.

options.title: No — keep location available
options.desc: Keeps location for maps, weather, and device recovery.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Windows location-aware features.

desktopQuestions.label: Ads & Suggestions
desktopQuestions.title: Stop Windows from personalizing ads and suggestions?
desktopQuestions.desc: Windows uses your activity to show targeted tips, app suggestions, and literal ads in the Start menu. Your OS is an ad platform. Let that sink in.

options.title: Yes — stop personalized suggestions
options.desc: Disables CloudContent, SoftLanding, and tailored experiences policies. No more surprise Candy Crush installs.

options.title: No — keep it
options.desc: Keeps personalized suggestions. Enjoy your Spotify ad in the Start menu.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep tailored experiences and app-launch personalization.

desktopQuestions.label: Voice & Typing
desktopQuestions.title: Disable voice and typing data collection?
desktopQuestions.desc: Windows can send your voice and typing patterns to Microsoft to improve their AI. If you don't use dictation, there's no reason for this.

options.title: Yes — disable it
options.desc: Stops sending voice and typing data to Microsoft.

options.title: No — keep it
options.desc: Keeps cloud speech recognition and input learning.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep speech and input personalization features.

desktopQuestions.label: SmartScreen
desktopQuestions.title: Disable SmartScreen download protection?
desktopQuestions.desc: SmartScreen checks files you download against Microsoft's database. It protects you, but also sends data about everything you download.
desktopQuestions.note: This is a real security tradeoff. Only disable if you're careful about what you download.

options.title: Yes — disable SmartScreen
options.desc: No more download scanning. You manage trust yourself.

warnings: Disabling SmartScreen can remove a useful download and reputation safety check.

options.title: No — keep SmartScreen
options.desc: Keeps download protection active. Recommended for most users.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep SmartScreen protection enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: Fast Startup
desktopQuestions.title: Disable Fast Startup?
desktopQuestions.desc: Fast Startup (HiberbootEnabled) hibernates the kernel instead of doing a full shutdown. This means drivers and services don't reinitialize, causing stale state bugs and dual-boot filesystem issues.

options.title: Yes — disable Fast Startup
options.desc: Sets HiberbootEnabled=0. Full shutdown/restart cycle. Drivers reinitialize properly. Fixes dual-boot NTFS corruption.

options.title: No — keep Fast Startup
options.desc: Keeps ~3-5 second faster boots at the cost of potential driver/filesystem quirks.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Fast Startup enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: Hibernation
desktopQuestions.title: Disable hibernation?
desktopQuestions.desc: Hibernation saves your session to disk when sleeping. It uses disk space equal to your RAM (8-64GB).

options.title: Yes — disable hibernation
options.desc: Frees disk space and ensures clean shutdowns.

options.title: No — keep hibernation
options.desc: Keeps hibernate as a sleep option.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep hibernation enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: Audio Effects
desktopQuestions.title: Turn off audio enhancements?
desktopQuestions.desc: Windows applies sound effects (bass boost, loudness equalization) that can distort audio. Turning them off gives you cleaner sound.

options.title: Yes — turn enhancements off
options.desc: Cleaner, more natural audio without processing effects.

warnings: Audio enhancements off can change loudness, EQ, and motherboard vendor DSP behavior.

options.title: No — keep enhancements
options.desc: Keeps audio effects like bass boost and virtual surround.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Windows audio enhancements enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: Exclusive Audio
desktopQuestions.title: Let apps take exclusive control of audio?
desktopQuestions.desc: This gives one app at a time direct access to your speakers for lower latency. Other apps won't make sound while it's active.

options.title: Yes — enable exclusive mode
options.desc: Better for music production and competitive gaming.

options.title: No — keep shared audio
options.desc: All apps share audio normally. Safer for everyday use.

behavior.blockReason: You chose not to enable exclusive-mode audio control.

desktopQuestions.label: Right-Click Menu
desktopQuestions.title: Clean up extra right-click menu clutter?
desktopQuestions.desc: Keeps the stock Windows 11 context menu style but removes a few noisy legacy entries using the safe Shell Extensions Blocked path only.

options.title: Yes — clean up extra entries
options.desc: Hides low-value items like Cast to Device and Paint 3D without using the crash-prone classic-menu hack.

options.title: No — keep every stock entry
options.desc: Leaves the default Windows context menu entries alone.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep the stock Windows context menu entries.

desktopQuestions.label: End Task
desktopQuestions.title: Add 'End Task' to taskbar right-click?
desktopQuestions.desc: When an app freezes, you can kill it directly from the taskbar instead of opening Task Manager.
desktopQuestions.note: Only shown on Windows builds that expose the taskbar End Task toggle.

options.title: Yes — add End Task
options.desc: Quick way to force-close frozen apps.

options.title: No — keep default taskbar menu
options.desc: Use Task Manager to close frozen apps as usual.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep the default taskbar right-click behavior.

desktopQuestions.label: Background Apps
desktopQuestions.title: Stop apps from running in the background?
desktopQuestions.desc: Many apps keep running silently even when you're not using them, eating CPU and memory.

options.title: Yes — stop background apps
options.desc: Apps only run when you actually open them.

options.title: No — keep background apps
options.desc: Keeps background app activity for live tiles, sync, and notifications.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep background app execution enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: Auto Maintenance
desktopQuestions.title: Disable automatic maintenance?
desktopQuestions.desc: Windows runs cleanup and optimization tasks when your PC is idle. This can cause unexpected activity and wake-ups.

options.title: Yes — disable it
options.desc: No surprise background maintenance. Your PC stays quiet when idle.

options.title: No — keep it
options.desc: Keeps automatic housekeeping. Good if you want Windows to self-maintain.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Windows automatic maintenance enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: Game Mode
desktopQuestions.title: Enable Game Mode?
desktopQuestions.desc: Game Mode tells Windows to prioritize games over background tasks. A small but real improvement.

options.title: Yes — enable Game Mode
options.desc: Windows gives games more resources when they're running.

options.title: No — keep default
options.desc: Keeps the current default behavior.

behavior.blockReason: You chose not to force Game Mode on.

desktopQuestions.label: Transparency
desktopQuestions.title: Disable transparency effects?
desktopQuestions.desc: The see-through blur effects on windows and taskbar use GPU resources. Turning them off makes the desktop feel snappier.

options.title: Yes — disable transparency
options.desc: Flat, clean look with better performance.

options.title: No — keep transparency
options.desc: Keeps the visual effects.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Windows transparency effects enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: CPU Boost
desktopQuestions.title: Use aggressive CPU boost?
desktopQuestions.desc: Keeps your CPU running at higher speeds under load instead of backing off to save power.

options.title: Yes — use aggressive boost
options.desc: More performance, but more heat and power consumption.

options.title: No — keep default boost
options.desc: Safer for thermals. Better for quiet operation.

behavior.blockReason: You chose not to force aggressive CPU boost behavior.

desktopQuestions.label: CPU Speed
desktopQuestions.title: Keep CPU speed at maximum?
desktopQuestions.desc: Prevents your CPU from slowing down between tasks. Reduces the tiny delays when the CPU needs to speed up again.

options.title: Yes — keep at full speed
options.desc: Fastest response times. CPU stays at full speed.

options.title: No — allow speed scaling
options.desc: CPU slows down when not busy. Better for efficiency and heat.

behavior.blockReason: You chose not to lock minimum processor state to 100%.

desktopQuestions.label: Core Parking
desktopQuestions.title: Keep all CPU cores active?
desktopQuestions.desc: Windows parks idle CPU cores (C6 state). Unparking takes 1-5ms which causes micro-stutters in games. Sets MinProcessorState to 100% and disables core parking via powercfg.

options.title: Yes — keep all cores active
options.desc: Sets processor idle disable, MinProcessorState=100%. All cores stay in C0. Eliminates core-unpark latency spikes.

options.title: No — allow core sleeping
options.desc: Cores enter C6 sleep when idle. Saves 5-15W power but adds wake latency.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep CPU core parking behavior.

desktopQuestions.label: Game Scheduling
desktopQuestions.title: Optimize multimedia scheduling for games?
desktopQuestions.desc: Adjusts how Windows schedules game and media threads to give them higher priority.

options.title: Yes — optimize for games
options.desc: Games and media get priority over background tasks.

options.title: No — keep defaults
options.desc: Keeps the default scheduling behavior.

behavior.blockReason: You chose not to harden the MMCSS gaming profile.

desktopQuestions.label: Memory
desktopQuestions.title: Optimize memory management?
desktopQuestions.desc: Disables memory compression (Disable-MMAgent -MemoryCompression), fixes the known NDU driver memory leak (ndu.sys Start=4), and disables SysMain/Superfetch service.

options.title: Yes — optimize memory
options.desc: Runs Disable-MMAgent, sets NDU Start=4, disables SysMain. Saves CPU overhead on 16GB+ systems. Fixes the ndu.sys leak that causes RAM usage to climb over uptime.

options.title: No — keep defaults
options.desc: Keeps Windows default memory handling. Memory compression and SysMain stay active.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Windows memory compression and related memory subsystem defaults.

desktopQuestions.label: GPU Scheduling
desktopQuestions.title: Disable Hardware Accelerated GPU Scheduling (HAGS)?
desktopQuestions.desc: HAGS (HwSchMode) offloads GPU memory scheduling to the GPU firmware. It reduces CPU overhead but can increase frame time variance and cause micro-stutters on some hardware.

options.title: Yes — disable HAGS
options.desc: Sets HwSchMode=1 in registry. More predictable frame times. Preferred for competitive/esports titles.

options.title: No — keep HAGS enabled
options.desc: Keeps HwSchMode=2. Can reduce CPU overhead but may cause inconsistent frame pacing.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep HAGS at the Windows default.

desktopQuestions.label: GPU Telemetry
desktopQuestions.title: Disable GPU driver telemetry?
desktopQuestions.desc: NVIDIA and AMD drivers run background services that collect usage data. Disabling them doesn't affect your graphics.

options.title: Yes — disable GPU telemetry
options.desc: Stops GPU driver analytics. Manual updates still work.

options.title: No — keep GPU telemetry
options.desc: Keeps GPU vendor telemetry running.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep GPU driver telemetry services active.

desktopQuestions.label: Game DVR
desktopQuestions.title: Disable Game DVR background recording?
desktopQuestions.desc: Game DVR (GameBarPresenceWriter + AppCaptureEnabled) silently records gameplay. It hooks into the GPU pipeline and causes measurable frame time spikes, especially on mid-range GPUs.

options.title: Yes — disable Game DVR
options.desc: Disables AppCaptureEnabled, GameDVR_Enabled, and GameBarPresenceWriter. Removes GPU pipeline hook. Measurable FPS improvement.

options.title: No — keep Game DVR
options.desc: Keeps built-in game recording. Alt+G to save last 30 seconds still works.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Game DVR behavior enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: Fullscreen
desktopQuestions.title: Disable fullscreen optimizations?
desktopQuestions.desc: Windows adds a compatibility layer to fullscreen apps. Disabling it gives you true fullscreen with lower input lag.

options.title: Yes — disable them
options.desc: True fullscreen mode. Lower input delay.

options.title: No — keep them enabled
options.desc: Keeps the Windows compatibility layer.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Windows fullscreen optimizations enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: Dynamic Tick
desktopQuestions.title: Disable dynamic tick for lower latency?
desktopQuestions.desc: Windows adjusts its internal timer dynamically. Fixing it gives more consistent timing, but uses slightly more power.
desktopQuestions.note: Advanced tweak. Best for dedicated gaming desktops only.

options.title: Yes — disable dynamic tick
options.desc: More consistent timing. Good for competitive gaming.

options.title: No — keep dynamic tick
options.desc: Keeps the power-saving timer behavior.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Windows dynamic tick enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: Edge Removal
desktopQuestions.title: Completely remove Microsoft Edge?
desktopQuestions.desc: This permanently deletes Edge from your system. This cannot be undone from within Windows.
desktopQuestions.note: Make sure you have another browser installed first. Some Windows features may break.

options.title: Yes — remove Edge
options.desc: Only for users who are 100% sure they don't need Edge.

warnings: Edge removal is irreversible without a manual reinstall.

options.title: No — keep Edge installed
options.desc: Keep Edge installed. The suppression options above are enough.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep the Edge browser installed.

desktopQuestions.label: WebView2
desktopQuestions.title: Keep WebView2 for app compatibility?
desktopQuestions.desc: WebView2 is a hidden component that many apps (Teams, Widgets, Electron apps) need to work. Removing it WILL break these apps.

options.title: Yes — keep WebView2
options.desc: Keep it. Most PCs need this for apps to work properly.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to preserve WebView2 for app compatibility.

options.title: No — remove WebView2
options.desc: Remove it. Only for stripped systems where you accept things may break.

warnings: Removing WebView2 can break Teams, installer UIs, and embedded web surfaces.

desktopQuestions.label: CPU Security
desktopQuestions.title: Reduce CPU security protections for more speed?
desktopQuestions.desc: Disabling Spectre mitigations can give 5-10% more performance, but removes important security protections.
desktopQuestions.note: Only consider this on isolated gaming PCs that don't browse the web or handle sensitive data.

options.title: Yes — reduce protections
options.desc: More performance, less security. For dedicated gaming rigs only.

warnings: Speculation mitigation reduction trades security for latency and syscall-heavy performance.

options.title: No — keep protections
options.desc: Keep security protections. Recommended for most systems.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep speculative execution mitigations intact.

desktopQuestions.label: Kernel Protection
desktopQuestions.title: Disable kernel code integrity protection?
desktopQuestions.desc: HVCI is a security feature that verifies code running in the Windows kernel. Disabling it may improve performance but weakens security.
desktopQuestions.note: Some anti-cheat systems (like Vanguard) may be affected.

options.title: Yes — disable HVCI
options.desc: For users who understand the kernel security tradeoff.

warnings: Disabling HVCI can break security baselines and some anti-cheat requirements.

options.title: No — keep HVCI enabled
options.desc: Keep the protection. Recommended.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Hypervisor-enforced Code Integrity enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: Windows Defender
desktopQuestions.title: Completely disable Windows Defender?
desktopQuestions.desc: This removes ALL antivirus protection. Your PC will have zero malware scanning. This is the most aggressive option in the entire wizard.
desktopQuestions.note: Cannot be easily re-enabled. Only for isolated gaming PCs that never browse or run unknown software.

options.title: Yes — disable Defender entirely
options.desc: No antivirus at all. Maximum performance, zero protection.

warnings: Disabling Defender removes all real-time malware protection.
Disabling VBS may break anti-cheat systems like Vanguard.

options.title: No — keep Defender active
options.desc: Keep Defender active. Strongly recommended.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Windows Defender and VBS active.

desktopQuestions.label: Windows Update
desktopQuestions.title: Stop all Windows updates?
desktopQuestions.desc: Disables Windows Update completely. You will NOT receive security patches, bug fixes, or driver updates unless you manually re-enable it.
desktopQuestions.note: This disables 5 services. It's the honest version of what many debloat tools do silently.

options.title: Yes — stop all updates
options.desc: Full control. No surprise downloads or reboots.

warnings: Stopping Windows Update means no security patches will be installed automatically.
You must manually re-enable these services to receive updates.

options.title: No — keep updates
options.desc: Keep updates. Security patches are important.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Windows Update services running.

desktopQuestions.label: Legacy Protocols
desktopQuestions.title: Disable insecure network protocols?
desktopQuestions.desc: Removes old, vulnerable network protocols (LLMNR, SMBv1, WPAD) that are commonly exploited in attacks.

options.title: Yes — disable legacy protocols
options.desc: Better security. Removes protocols used in real-world hacks.

warnings: Disabling legacy protocols may affect old NAS devices or file-sharing setups.

options.title: No — keep legacy protocols
options.desc: Keep for compatibility with very old network devices.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep LLMNR and SMBv1 legacy protocols enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: Teredo
desktopQuestions.title: Disable Teredo tunneling?
desktopQuestions.desc: Teredo is an old workaround for IPv6 compatibility. Almost nothing uses it anymore.

options.title: Yes — disable Teredo
options.desc: Removes an outdated network layer. Safe for almost everyone.

warnings: Aggressive network tuning is hardware-sensitive. Verify games, VPNs, and voice chat after apply.

options.title: No — keep Teredo
options.desc: Leave it if you're not sure.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Teredo tunneling enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: NetBIOS
desktopQuestions.title: Disable NetBIOS?
desktopQuestions.desc: NetBIOS is an old way for computers to find each other on a network. Modern networks don't need it, but very old printers or NAS devices might.

options.title: Yes — disable NetBIOS
options.desc: Removes an old protocol. Safe unless you have very old network devices.

warnings: Aggressive network tuning is hardware-sensitive. Verify games, VPNs, and voice chat after apply.

options.title: No — keep NetBIOS
options.desc: Safer if you have old network equipment.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep NetBIOS over TCP/IP enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: Network Latency
desktopQuestions.title: Disable Nagle's algorithm for lower network latency?
desktopQuestions.desc: Nagle's algorithm batches small TCP packets to reduce overhead, but adds 40-200ms delay per send. Disabling it (TcpAckFrequency=1, TCPNoDelay=1) sends each packet immediately. Important for competitive gaming and VoIP.
desktopQuestions.note: Registry: HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\Tcpip\Parameters\Interfaces\{adapter}. Applied per-adapter.

options.title: Yes — disable packet batching
options.desc: Sets TcpAckFrequency=1 and TCPNoDelay=1 on all interfaces. Each packet fires immediately. Measurable ping reduction in online games.

warnings: Aggressive network tuning is hardware-sensitive. Verify games, VPNs, and voice chat after apply.

options.title: No — keep packet buffering
options.desc: Keeps Nagle's algorithm. Better throughput for bulk transfers and downloads.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep the default TCP packet coalescing behavior.

desktopQuestions.label: Network Adapter
desktopQuestions.title: Switch to low-latency network settings?
desktopQuestions.desc: Lets your CPU handle network processing directly instead of your network card. Faster for gaming, but uses slightly more CPU.

options.title: Yes — low-latency mode
options.desc: Lower network delay. Best for wired gaming setups.

warnings: Aggressive network tuning is hardware-sensitive. Verify games, VPNs, and voice chat after apply.

options.title: No — keep default settings
options.desc: Safer for Wi-Fi, downloads, and general use.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep default NIC offloading and queue behavior.

desktopQuestions.label: Update Sharing
desktopQuestions.title: Stop Windows from sharing updates with other PCs?
desktopQuestions.desc: Windows uploads parts of updates to other computers, using your internet bandwidth in the background.

options.title: Yes — disable it
options.desc: Your bandwidth stays yours. No background uploading.

options.title: No — keep it
options.desc: Lets Windows share updates to help other PCs download faster.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Delivery Optimization enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: USB Power
desktopQuestions.title: Stop Windows from putting USB devices to sleep?
desktopQuestions.desc: Windows puts USB devices to sleep to save power. This can cause your mouse, keyboard, or headset to briefly disconnect.

options.title: Yes — keep USB devices awake
options.desc: No more USB disconnects or wake-up delays.

options.title: No — keep USB power saving
options.desc: Saves a bit of power. Better for laptops.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep USB selective suspend enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: PCIe Power
desktopQuestions.title: Disable PCIe power saving?
desktopQuestions.desc: Your GPU and SSD connect through PCIe. Power saving mode adds tiny delays when they wake up from idle. On desktops, disabling this is safe.

options.title: Yes — disable PCIe power saving
options.desc: Faster response from GPU and SSD. No wake-up delays.

options.title: No — keep PCIe power saving
options.desc: Saves a small amount of power. Better for laptops.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep PCIe link-state power management enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: Mouse Precision
desktopQuestions.title: Disable mouse acceleration for raw input?
desktopQuestions.desc: Windows applies pointer acceleration (EnhancedPointerPrecision) that scales mouse movement non-linearly. Competitive gamers need 1:1 input where physical distance = cursor distance, regardless of speed.
desktopQuestions.note: EnhancedPointerPrecision applies a non-linear curve that makes muscle memory inconsistent. Every competitive FPS player disables this.

options.title: Yes — disable acceleration
options.desc: Sets MouseSpeed=0, MouseThreshold1=0, MouseThreshold2=0 in HKCU\Control Panel\Mouse. Pure 1:1 mouse input. Your aim is finally yours.

options.title: No — keep acceleration
options.desc: Keeps Windows pointer acceleration. Fine for general desktop use.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Windows mouse acceleration enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: Sticky Keys
desktopQuestions.title: Disable Sticky Keys and related shortcuts?
desktopQuestions.desc: Pressing Shift 5 times triggers a Sticky Keys popup. Filter Keys and Toggle Keys have similar shortcuts. Every gamer has been interrupted by this at least once during the worst possible moment.
desktopQuestions.note: Registry: HKCU\Control Panel\Accessibility\StickyKeys (Flags="506"). This only disables the keyboard shortcuts, not the features themselves if enabled through Settings.

options.title: Yes — disable accessibility shortcuts
options.desc: Disables Sticky Keys (Flags=506), Filter Keys, and Toggle Keys activation shortcuts. No more surprise popups mid-game.

options.title: No — keep accessibility shortcuts
options.desc: Keeps Shift-5x, right Shift hold, and Num Lock shortcuts active.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep accessibility keyboard shortcuts active.

desktopQuestions.label: FTH
desktopQuestions.title: Disable Fault Tolerant Heap?
desktopQuestions.desc: FTH monitors crashing apps and silently applies heap mitigations (extra padding, delayed frees) to "fix" them. This adds memory overhead and hides real bugs instead of fixing them. Gamers don't need Windows babysitting their heap.
desktopQuestions.note: FTH was designed for enterprises running legacy apps. On a gaming PC, the overhead is pure waste.

options.title: Yes — disable FTH
options.desc: Sets HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\FTH → Enabled=0. Removes hidden heap overhead. Apps crash honestly instead of running slowly.

options.title: No — keep FTH enabled
options.desc: Keeps Windows auto-fixing unstable apps with heap padding.

desktopQuestions.label: Multi-Plane Overlays
desktopQuestions.title: Disable Multi-Plane Overlays (MPOs)?
desktopQuestions.desc: MPOs (iFlip) let the GPU compositor use hardware overlay planes for rendering. On some GPU/monitor combos, this causes frame pacing issues, black screen flickers, and inconsistent frame times. Disabling forces classic single-plane composition.
desktopQuestions.note: Known to cause issues with mixed refresh rate multi-monitor setups and certain NVIDIA/AMD driver versions. Disabling is a common fix for stuttering.

options.title: Yes — disable MPOs
options.desc: Sets OverlayTestMode=5 in HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\Dwm. Forces classic composition. Fixes frame pacing on affected hardware.

options.title: No — keep MPOs enabled
options.desc: Keeps hardware overlay planes. Works fine on most hardware.

desktopQuestions.label: File Timestamps
desktopQuestions.title: Disable NTFS last access timestamps?
desktopQuestions.desc: NTFS updates the last access time on every file read. That's a write operation for every read — an absurd default. Disabling it reduces disk writes noticeably on HDDs and marginally on SSDs.
desktopQuestions.note: Windows Server disables this by default. The fact that desktop Windows doesn't tells you everything about Microsoft's priorities.

options.title: Yes — disable last access timestamps
options.desc: Runs fsutil behavior set disablelastaccess 1. Fewer disk writes on every file open. Windows Server has this disabled by default — desktop Windows just forgot.

options.title: No — keep timestamps
options.desc: Keeps NTFS last access time updates. Needed for some backup and audit tools.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep NTFS last access timestamps enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: Device Power Saving
desktopQuestions.title: Disable power saving on USB, NIC, and PCI devices?
desktopQuestions.desc: Windows puts USB controllers, network adapters, and PCI devices into low-power states when idle. This adds wake latency (1-10ms) that causes input lag, network hiccups, and audio dropouts on desktops.
desktopQuestions.note: Uses WMI MSPower_DeviceEnable to disable power management per-device. Only makes sense on desktops — laptops need this for battery life.

options.title: Yes — keep all devices fully powered
options.desc: Disables MSPower_DeviceEnable power management on USB, NIC, and PCI devices. Eliminates device wake latency. Your peripherals never sleep.

options.title: No — keep device power saving
options.desc: Lets Windows put idle devices to sleep. Saves a few watts on desktops.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep device power management enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: Flip Mode
desktopQuestions.title: Force Legacy Flip (true exclusive fullscreen)?
desktopQuestions.desc: Modern Windows uses composed flip (DWM adds a frame of latency). Legacy Flip bypasses DWM entirely for the lowest possible input-to-display latency. This is what competitive gamers use.
desktopQuestions.note: Registry: HKCU\System\GameConfigStore. Legacy Flip breaks some overlay software (Discord, Steam). Test per-game.

options.title: Yes — force Legacy Flip
options.desc: Sets GameDVR_DXGIHonorFSEWindowsCompatible=1 and GameDVR_FSEBehavior=2. Bypasses DWM composition. Lowest possible display latency.

options.title: No — keep composed flip
options.desc: Keeps modern Windows flip model. More compatible with overlays and alt-tab.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep modern composed flip. Compatible with overlays.

desktopQuestions.label: Store Updates
desktopQuestions.title: Disable automatic Microsoft Store app updates?
desktopQuestions.desc: Microsoft Store downloads and installs app updates in the background without asking. This consumes bandwidth, CPU, and can happen during gaming sessions.
desktopQuestions.note: Registry: HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\WindowsStore. Manual updates still work — you just choose when.

options.title: Yes — disable auto-updates
options.desc: Sets AutoDownload=2 policy. Store apps won't update automatically. You can still update manually from the Store.

options.title: No — keep auto-updates
options.desc: Keeps automatic Store app updates. Apps stay current without manual intervention.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Store auto-updates enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: Auto Maintenance
desktopQuestions.title: Disable Windows automatic maintenance?
desktopQuestions.desc: Windows runs defrag, security scans, and cleanup tasks on a schedule. These tasks can cause sudden CPU/disk spikes during gaming or work, and wake your PC from sleep.
desktopQuestions.note: Registry: HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Schedule\Maintenance. Run 'cleanmgr' manually when needed.

options.title: Yes — disable auto maintenance
options.desc: Sets MaintenanceDisabled=1. No more surprise defrag, security scans, or cleanup during sessions. You run maintenance when YOU choose.

options.title: No — keep auto maintenance
options.desc: Keeps the automatic housekeeping schedule. Good if you want Windows to self-maintain.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep automatic maintenance. Windows will self-maintain.

desktopQuestions.label: Full Defender Kill
desktopQuestions.title: Completely disable Windows Defender at the service level?
desktopQuestions.desc: The nuclear option. Disables WinDefend, WdFilter, WdBoot, WdNisSvc, SecurityHealthService, wscsvc, Sense, MsSecCore — all 9 Defender services plus SmartScreen, WTDS, and cloud protection. Zero antivirus. Zero overhead. You must disable Tamper Protection in Windows Security settings first.
desktopQuestions.note: Requires Tamper Protection to be manually disabled first (Settings → Windows Security → Virus & Threat Protection → Manage Settings). Without this, service changes will be reverted on reboot.

options.title: Yes — kill Defender completely
options.desc: Disables 9 services, 12+ registry keys. MsMpEng.exe gone forever. ~500MB RAM freed, measurable FPS improvement. Zero protection.

options.title: No — keep Defender running
options.desc: Keeps Defender real-time protection. Recommended for any internet-connected PC.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Windows Defender active. Smart choice for internet-connected PCs.

desktopQuestions.label: Driver Blocklist
desktopQuestions.title: Disable the Vulnerable Driver Blocklist?
desktopQuestions.desc: Microsoft blocks drivers with known vulnerabilities from loading. Disabling this allows kernel tools like RW-Everything (used for XHCI IMOD tuning) and older hardware drivers that Microsoft has blacklisted.
desktopQuestions.note: Registry: HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\CI\Config. Only disable if you specifically need a blocked tool like RW-Everything.

options.title: Yes — allow all drivers
options.desc: Sets VulnerableDriverBlocklistEnable=0. Required for RW-Everything, MSI Utility, and some legacy hardware. You're responsible for driver safety.

options.title: No — keep the blocklist
options.desc: Keeps Microsoft's driver blocklist active. Safer but blocks some performance tuning tools.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep the driver blocklist. Blocked drivers stay blocked.

desktopQuestions.label: CPU Mitigations
desktopQuestions.title: Disable Spectre/Meltdown CPU security mitigations?
desktopQuestions.desc: Modern CPUs have hardware mitigations for Spectre V1/V2/V4, Meltdown, MDS, and L1TF. These add 2-15% performance overhead on syscall-heavy workloads. Disabling them renames the microcode update DLL (requires TrustedInstaller) and sets FeatureSettingsOverride=3.
desktopQuestions.note: Requires TrustedInstaller elevation. Exposes system to Spectre, Meltdown, MDS, L1TF. Only for air-gapped dedicated gaming PCs. The DLL rename is reversed by renaming back.

options.title: Yes — disable CPU mitigations
options.desc: Renames mcupdate_GenuineIntel.dll / mcupdate_AuthenticAMD.dll and sets FeatureSettings to disable all mitigations. 2-15% perf gain on CPU-bound workloads.

options.title: No — keep mitigations active
options.desc: Keeps all CPU security mitigations. Recommended for any system handling sensitive data.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep CPU security mitigations active. Safer, with minor performance cost.

featurePages.description: This decides how many changes we make. Start safe, or go deeper if you know what you're doing.

options.name: aggressionPreset__conservative
options.desc: Light cleanup and privacy fixes only. Nothing risky. Best if you're not sure.

options.name: aggressionPreset__balanced
options.desc: Good mix of performance and privacy improvements. Works well for most PCs.

options.name: aggressionPreset__aggressive
options.desc: Deeper tweaks for gaming PCs and power users. May change how some features work.

options.name: aggressionPreset__expert
options.desc: Unlocks all options including security tradeoffs. Only for users who know exactly what they want.

featurePages.description: This stops Windows from slowing down your CPU to save power. Your PC will respond faster. Sets powercfg to High Performance scheme and adjusts processor idle states.

options.name: highPerformancePlan__true
options.desc: CPU stays at higher frequencies. Eliminates 1-5ms wake latency from C-states. Measurable in frame time graphs.

options.name: highPerformancePlan__false
options.desc: Keeps Balanced plan with CPU frequency scaling. Better for laptops on battery.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Windows balanced power behavior.

featurePages.description: Adjusts Win32PrioritySeparation to favor foreground processes. Makes the scheduler give your active app (game, editor) more quantum time than background services.

options.name: optimizeThreadPriority__false
options.desc: Keeps default Win32PrioritySeparation. Background tasks get equal scheduling.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep default Windows thread scheduling behavior.

featurePages.description: Windows 11 changed timer resolution to per-process (24H2+). This sets GlobalTimerResolutionRequests=1 to restore the global 0.5ms timer that games rely on for consistent frame pacing.

options.name: globalTimerResolution__true

options.name: globalTimerResolution__false
options.desc: Keeps per-process timer. Some games may have inconsistent frame times.

options.desc: Printer support stays enabled.

featurePages.description: Edge runs 3-5 background processes even when closed, pre-loads at boot, and nags you to switch from your preferred browser. It's like that friend who won't leave your party.

options.desc: Leave Edge completely untouched.

featurePages.description: Copilot is Microsoft's AI assistant wedged into the taskbar. Uses 150-300MB RAM sitting idle and phones home to Bing. Nobody asked for this.

featurePages.description: Recall takes screenshots of your screen every few seconds so AI can search your history. Yes, really. It stores everything locally but the concept is... concerning.

options.name: disableRecall__false

featurePages.description: Click to Do is a Windows 11 AI feature that analyzes what's on your screen and suggests actions.

options.name: disableClickToDo__true

options.name: disableClickToDo__false
options.desc: Keeps Click to Do available.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Click to Do available.

featurePages.description: Microsoft added AI to several built-in apps. These send your data to the cloud for processing.

options.name: disableAiApps__true

options.name: disableAiApps__false
options.desc: Keeps AI features available in built-in apps.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep AI features in system apps.

featurePages.description: Windows sends usage data, app crash reports, typing/inking data, and browsing patterns to Microsoft via DiagTrack service and scheduled tasks.

options.name: telemetryLevel__keep
options.desc: Leave all data collection at Windows defaults. DiagTrack stays running.

behavior.blockReason: You chose not to reduce Microsoft telemetry in this area.

options.name: telemetryLevel__reduce

options.name: telemetryLevel__aggressive

featurePages.description: Windows can save everything you copy and sync it across devices. If you don't use this, it's just extra tracking.

options.desc: Clipboard works normally but doesn't save history or sync.

options.name: disableClipboardHistory__false
options.desc: Keeps clipboard history and cross-device sync.

featurePages.description: Windows tracks what files you open and apps you use, then shows this in a 'timeline'. This data is also sent to the cloud.

options.name: disableActivityFeed__false

featurePages.description: Windows tracks your location for maps, weather, and Find My Device. On a desktop, this is usually unnecessary.

options.name: disableLocation__false
options.desc: Keeps location for maps, weather, and device recovery.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Windows location-aware features.

featurePages.description: Windows uses your activity to show targeted tips, app suggestions, and literal ads in the Start menu. Your OS is an ad platform. Let that sink in.

options.name: disableTailoredExperiences__true
options.desc: Disables CloudContent, SoftLanding, and tailored experiences policies. No more surprise Candy Crush installs.

options.name: disableTailoredExperiences__false
options.desc: Keeps personalized suggestions. Enjoy your Spotify ad in the Start menu.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep tailored experiences and app-launch personalization.

featurePages.description: Windows can send your voice and typing patterns to Microsoft to improve their AI. If you don't use dictation, there's no reason for this.

options.desc: Stops sending voice and typing data to Microsoft.

options.name: disableSpeechPersonalization__false

featurePages.description: SmartScreen checks files you download against Microsoft's database. It protects you, but also sends data about everything you download.

options.name: disableHibernation__false
options.desc: Keeps hibernate as a sleep option.

featurePages.description: Windows adjusts its internal timer dynamically. Fixing it gives more consistent timing, but uses slightly more power.

options.name: disableDynamicTick__true
options.desc: More consistent timing. Good for competitive gaming.

options.name: disableDynamicTick__false
options.desc: Keeps the power-saving timer behavior.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Windows dynamic tick enabled.

featurePages.description: This permanently deletes Edge from your system. This cannot be undone from within Windows.

options.name: removeEdge__true
options.desc: Only for users who are 100% sure they don't need Edge.

warnings: Edge removal is irreversible without a manual reinstall.

options.name: removeEdge__false
options.desc: Keep Edge installed. The suppression options above are enough.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep the Edge browser installed.

featurePages.description: WebView2 is a hidden component that many apps (Teams, Widgets, Electron apps) need to work. Removing it WILL break these apps.

options.name: preserveWebView2__true
options.desc: Keep it. Most PCs need this for apps to work properly.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to preserve WebView2 for app compatibility.

options.name: preserveWebView2__false
options.desc: Remove it. Only for stripped systems where you accept things may break.

warnings: Removing WebView2 can break Teams, installer UIs, and embedded web surfaces.

featurePages.description: Disabling Spectre mitigations can give 5-10% more performance, but removes important security protections.

options.name: reduceMitigations__true
options.desc: More performance, less security. For dedicated gaming rigs only.

warnings: Speculation mitigation reduction trades security for latency and syscall-heavy performance.

options.name: reduceMitigations__false
options.desc: Keep security protections. Recommended for most systems.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep speculative execution mitigations intact.

featurePages.description: HVCI is a security feature that verifies code running in the Windows kernel. Disabling it may improve performance but weakens security.

options.name: disableHvci__true
options.desc: For users who understand the kernel security tradeoff.

warnings: Disabling HVCI can break security baselines and some anti-cheat requirements.

options.name: disableHvci__false
options.desc: Keep the protection. Recommended.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Hypervisor-enforced Code Integrity enabled.

featurePages.description: This removes ALL antivirus protection. Your PC will have zero malware scanning. This is the most aggressive option in the entire wizard.

options.name: disableDefender__true
options.desc: No antivirus at all. Maximum performance, zero protection.

warnings: Disabling Defender removes all real-time malware protection.
Disabling VBS may break anti-cheat systems like Vanguard.

options.name: disableDefender__false
options.desc: Keep Defender active. Strongly recommended.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Windows Defender and VBS active.

featurePages.description: Disables Windows Update completely. You will NOT receive security patches, bug fixes, or driver updates unless you manually re-enable it.

options.name: disableWindowsUpdate__true
options.desc: Full control. No surprise downloads or reboots.

warnings: Stopping Windows Update means no security patches will be installed automatically.
You must manually re-enable these services to receive updates.

options.name: disableWindowsUpdate__false
options.desc: Keep updates. Security patches are important.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Windows Update services running.

featurePages.description: Removes old, vulnerable network protocols (LLMNR, SMBv1, WPAD) that are commonly exploited in attacks.

options.name: disableLlmnr__true
options.desc: Better security. Removes protocols used in real-world hacks.

warnings: Disabling legacy protocols may affect old NAS devices or file-sharing setups.

options.name: disableLlmnr__false
options.desc: Keep for compatibility with very old network devices.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep LLMNR and SMBv1 legacy protocols enabled.

featurePages.description: Teredo is an old workaround for IPv6 compatibility. Almost nothing uses it anymore.

options.name: disableTeredo__true
options.desc: Removes an outdated network layer. Safe for almost everyone.

warnings: Aggressive network tuning is hardware-sensitive. Verify games, VPNs, and voice chat after apply.

options.name: disableTeredo__false
options.desc: Leave it if you're not sure.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Teredo tunneling enabled.

featurePages.description: NetBIOS is an old way for computers to find each other on a network. Modern networks don't need it, but very old printers or NAS devices might.

options.name: disableNetbios__true
options.desc: Removes an old protocol. Safe unless you have very old network devices.

warnings: Aggressive network tuning is hardware-sensitive. Verify games, VPNs, and voice chat after apply.

options.name: disableNetbios__false
options.desc: Safer if you have old network equipment.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep NetBIOS over TCP/IP enabled.

featurePages.description: Nagle's algorithm batches small TCP packets to reduce overhead, but adds 40-200ms delay per send. Disabling it (TcpAckFrequency=1, TCPNoDelay=1) sends each packet immediately. Important for competitive gaming and VoIP.

options.name: disableNagle__true
options.desc: Sets TcpAckFrequency=1 and TCPNoDelay=1 on all interfaces. Each packet fires immediately. Measurable ping reduction in online games.

warnings: Aggressive network tuning is hardware-sensitive. Verify games, VPNs, and voice chat after apply.

options.name: disableNagle__false
options.desc: Keeps Nagle's algorithm. Better throughput for bulk transfers and downloads.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep the default TCP packet coalescing behavior.

featurePages.description: Lets your CPU handle network processing directly instead of your network card. Faster for gaming, but uses slightly more CPU.

options.name: disableNicOffloading__true
options.desc: Lower network delay. Best for wired gaming setups.

warnings: Aggressive network tuning is hardware-sensitive. Verify games, VPNs, and voice chat after apply.

options.name: disableNicOffloading__false
options.desc: Safer for Wi-Fi, downloads, and general use.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep default NIC offloading and queue behavior.

featurePages.description: Windows uploads parts of updates to other computers, using your internet bandwidth in the background.

options.name: disableDeliveryOptimization__true
options.desc: Your bandwidth stays yours. No background uploading.

options.name: disableDeliveryOptimization__false
options.desc: Lets Windows share updates to help other PCs download faster.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Delivery Optimization enabled.

featurePages.description: Windows puts USB devices to sleep to save power. This can cause your mouse, keyboard, or headset to briefly disconnect.

options.name: disableUsbSelectiveSuspend__true
options.desc: No more USB disconnects or wake-up delays.

options.name: disableUsbSelectiveSuspend__false
options.desc: Saves a bit of power. Better for laptops.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep USB selective suspend enabled.

featurePages.description: Your GPU and SSD connect through PCIe. Power saving mode adds tiny delays when they wake up from idle. On desktops, disabling this is safe.

options.name: disablePcieLinkStatePm__true
options.desc: Faster response from GPU and SSD. No wake-up delays.

options.name: disablePcieLinkStatePm__false
options.desc: Saves a small amount of power. Better for laptops.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep PCIe link-state power management enabled.

featurePages.description: Windows applies pointer acceleration (EnhancedPointerPrecision) that scales mouse movement non-linearly. Competitive gamers need 1:1 input where physical distance = cursor distance, regardless of speed.

options.name: disableMouseAcceleration__true
options.desc: Sets MouseSpeed=0, MouseThreshold1=0, MouseThreshold2=0 in HKCU\Control Panel\Mouse. Pure 1:1 mouse input. Your aim is finally yours.

options.name: disableMouseAcceleration__false
options.desc: Keeps Windows pointer acceleration. Fine for general desktop use.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Windows mouse acceleration enabled.

featurePages.description: Pressing Shift 5 times triggers a Sticky Keys popup. Filter Keys and Toggle Keys have similar shortcuts. Every gamer has been interrupted by this at least once during the worst possible moment.

options.name: disableStickyKeys__true
options.desc: Disables Sticky Keys (Flags=506), Filter Keys, and Toggle Keys activation shortcuts. No more surprise popups mid-game.

options.name: disableStickyKeys__false
options.desc: Keeps Shift-5x, right Shift hold, and Num Lock shortcuts active.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep accessibility keyboard shortcuts active.

featurePages.description: FTH monitors crashing apps and silently applies heap mitigations (extra padding, delayed frees) to "fix" them. This adds memory overhead and hides real bugs instead of fixing them. Gamers don't need Windows babysitting their heap.

options.name: disableFaultTolerantHeap__true
options.desc: Sets HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\FTH → Enabled=0. Removes hidden heap overhead. Apps crash honestly instead of running slowly.

options.name: disableFaultTolerantHeap__false
options.desc: Keeps Windows auto-fixing unstable apps with heap padding.

featurePages.description: MPOs (iFlip) let the GPU compositor use hardware overlay planes for rendering. On some GPU/monitor combos, this causes frame pacing issues, black screen flickers, and inconsistent frame times. Disabling forces classic single-plane composition.

options.name: disableMPOs__true
options.desc: Sets OverlayTestMode=5 in HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\Dwm. Forces classic composition. Fixes frame pacing on affected hardware.

options.name: disableMPOs__false
options.desc: Keeps hardware overlay planes. Works fine on most hardware.

featurePages.description: NTFS updates the last access time on every file read. That's a write operation for every read — an absurd default. Disabling it reduces disk writes noticeably on HDDs and marginally on SSDs.

options.name: disableLastAccessTime__true
options.desc: Runs fsutil behavior set disablelastaccess 1. Fewer disk writes on every file open. Windows Server has this disabled by default — desktop Windows just forgot.

options.name: disableLastAccessTime__false
options.desc: Keeps NTFS last access time updates. Needed for some backup and audit tools.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep NTFS last access timestamps enabled.

featurePages.description: Windows puts USB controllers, network adapters, and PCI devices into low-power states when idle. This adds wake latency (1-10ms) that causes input lag, network hiccups, and audio dropouts on desktops.

options.text: Yes — keep all devices fully powered
options.name: disableDevicePowerSaving__true
options.desc: Disables MSPower_DeviceEnable power management on USB, NIC, and PCI devices. Eliminates device wake latency. Your peripherals never sleep.

options.name: disableDevicePowerSaving__false
options.desc: Lets Windows put idle devices to sleep. Saves a few watts on desktops.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep device power management enabled.

featurePages.description: Modern Windows uses composed flip (DWM adds a frame of latency). Legacy Flip bypasses DWM entirely for the lowest possible input-to-display latency. This is what competitive gamers use.

options.name: legacyFlipPresentation__true
options.desc: Sets GameDVR_DXGIHonorFSEWindowsCompatible=1 and GameDVR_FSEBehavior=2. Bypasses DWM composition. Lowest possible display latency.

options.name: legacyFlipPresentation__false
options.desc: Keeps modern Windows flip model. More compatible with overlays and alt-tab.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep modern composed flip. Compatible with overlays.

featurePages.description: Microsoft Store downloads and installs app updates in the background without asking. This consumes bandwidth, CPU, and can happen during gaming sessions.

options.name: disableStoreAutoUpdates__true
options.desc: Sets AutoDownload=2 policy. Store apps won't update automatically. You can still update manually from the Store.

options.name: disableStoreAutoUpdates__false
options.desc: Keeps automatic Store app updates. Apps stay current without manual intervention.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Store auto-updates enabled.

featurePages.description: Windows runs defrag, security scans, and cleanup tasks on a schedule. These tasks can cause sudden CPU/disk spikes during gaming or work, and wake your PC from sleep.

options.name: disableAutoMaintenance__true
options.desc: Sets MaintenanceDisabled=1. No more surprise defrag, security scans, or cleanup during sessions. You run maintenance when YOU choose.

options.name: disableAutoMaintenance__false
options.desc: Keeps the automatic housekeeping schedule. Good if you want Windows to self-maintain.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep automatic maintenance. Windows will self-maintain.

featurePages.description: The nuclear option. Disables WinDefend, WdFilter, WdBoot, WdNisSvc, SecurityHealthService, wscsvc, Sense, MsSecCore — all 9 Defender services plus SmartScreen, WTDS, and cloud protection. Zero antivirus. Zero overhead. You must disable Tamper Protection in Windows Security settings first.

options.name: fullDefenderDisable__true
options.desc: Disables 9 services, 12+ registry keys. MsMpEng.exe gone forever. ~500MB RAM freed, measurable FPS improvement. Zero protection.

options.name: fullDefenderDisable__false
options.desc: Keeps Defender real-time protection. Recommended for any internet-connected PC.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Windows Defender active. Smart choice for internet-connected PCs.

featurePages.description: Microsoft blocks drivers with known vulnerabilities from loading. Disabling this allows kernel tools like RW-Everything (used for XHCI IMOD tuning) and older hardware drivers that Microsoft has blacklisted.

options.name: disableVulnerableDriverBlocklist__true
options.desc: Sets VulnerableDriverBlocklistEnable=0. Required for RW-Everything, MSI Utility, and some legacy hardware. You're responsible for driver safety.

options.name: disableVulnerableDriverBlocklist__false
options.desc: Keeps Microsoft's driver blocklist active. Safer but blocks some performance tuning tools.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep the driver blocklist. Blocked drivers stay blocked.

featurePages.description: Modern CPUs have hardware mitigations for Spectre V1/V2/V4, Meltdown, MDS, and L1TF. These add 2-15% performance overhead on syscall-heavy workloads. Disabling them renames the microcode update DLL (requires TrustedInstaller) and sets FeatureSettingsOverride=3.

options.name: disableCpuMitigations__true
options.desc: Renames mcupdate_GenuineIntel.dll / mcupdate_AuthenticAMD.dll and sets FeatureSettings to disable all mitigations. 2-15% perf gain on CPU-bound workloads.

options.name: disableCpuMitigations__false
options.desc: Keeps all CPU security mitigations. Recommended for any system handling sensitive data.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep CPU security mitigations active. Safer, with minor performance cost.
